retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Trachea Epiglottic Vallecula -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Lymphatic drainage in the area of the vallecula includes both tonsils and lymph nodes. The palatine tonsil and the lingual tonsils are within a few centimeters of the vallecula. There is also an extensive amount of lymphatic tissue called the lateral bands of the pharynx in this region. Combined, these tonsils and the lateral bands make up Waldeyer's ring, or nasal-associated lymphoid tissue (NALT). The deep cervical lymph nodes are the primary drainage of the vallecular region. These lymph nodes drain to the jugulofacial venous junction, which continues to drain inferiorly to the jugulo-subclavian venous junction bilaterally. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Laryngopharynx -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Pharyngeal constrictors surround the pharynx posteriorly and laterally, meeting midline at the posterior raphe. The pharyngeal constrictors are comprised of three circularly oriented muscles, the superior, medial, and inferior constrictors, that function to propel food and water from the oropharynx through the laryngopharynx, and on to the esophagus. Although oriented circularly around the pharynx, constrictors are arranged longitudinally, which aids in maintaining the airway within the pharynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Retropharyngeal Space -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The longus colli muscles serve as an important landmark for the retropharyngeal space as they mark the location of the medial group of retropharyngeal lymph nodes from the lateral group of nodes. The medial lymph nodes are anterior to the longus colli muscle, and the lateral lymph nodes are ventral to the muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Tonsils -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The palatine tonsils lie along the lateral wall of the oropharynx, situated in a fossa between the anterior and posterior pillars. Five arteries contribute to the vascular supply of these structures. The primary source is the tonsillar branch of the facial artery, which enters at the lower pole. Additional arterial input arises from the ascending palatine, dorsal lingual, ascending pharyngeal, and lesser palatine arteries. [8] Venous drainage occurs mainly via the peritonsillar venous plexus, which empties into the pharyngeal and lingual veins before reaching the internal jugular vein.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Retropharyngeal Space -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The blood supply to the pharynx varies based on anatomic location. The superior pharynx receives its blood supply from the ascending pharyngeal artery and the lesser palatine arteries. The inferior pharynx’s blood supply comes from the superior and inferior thyroid arteries. Another significant relationship is the proximity of the carotid sheath (internal carotid artery, common carotid artery, internal jugular vein, and vagus nerve) to the retropharyngeal space, composing the lateral border of the region.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx -- Structure and Function -- Laryngeal Cavity
retrieved_docs.content: The posterior part of the internal space of the larynx is part of the anterior wall of the pharynx and has two vertical recesses referred to as the piriform sinus. The form of the lateral aspects is determined by the larynx cartilage and consists of three parts: a superior one that matches the thyroid cartilage, an inferior one that matches the cricoid cartilage, and a middle one called the cricothyroid space. The Larynx apex forms a hole that joins to the trachea. The base of the larynx is oval-shaped and communicates with the pharynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Clinical Significance -- Obstruction Of The Airway
retrieved_docs.content: Ludwig angina infection, once established, evolves to include the tongue. The tongue may enlarge to two or three times its usual size and tends to distend posteriorly into the hypopharynx, superiorly against the palate, and anteriorly out of the oral cavity. Any immediate posterior extension of this process will ultimately involve the epiglottis. The styloglossus muscle creates the connection between the submandibular parapharyngeal spaces, otherwise known as the buccopharyngeal gap, as it leaves the tongue and passes in between the middle and superior constrictor muscles before attaching to the styloid process. Cellulitis of the submandibular space may spread into the pharyngeal space and, from there, into the retropharyngeal space of the mediastinum. The tongue plays a major role in breathing; as such, it is involved in the collapse of the upper airway in obstructive sleep apnoea syndrome patients. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pharynx -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Regionally, the pharynx divides into three parts which are from superior to inferior:-the nasal pharynx, located behind the posterior nasal apertures (choanae), the oral pharynx, located behind the opening of the oral cavity, and the laryngeal pharynx, located behind the inlet (opening) of the larynx. First, the nasal pharynx is only related to the respiratory tract as air passes through it from the nasal cavities. Also, within the lateral surface of the back of the nasopharynx, there are two openings, one on either side, which is called the auditory tubes (Eustachian tubes or pharyngotympanic tubes) surrounded by elevations of mucous membrane called tubal elevations. These tubes are connected to the middle ears (tympanic cavities) posteriorly and mainly serve to equalize pressure and facilitate the drainage of the secretions of the middle ears. Second, the oral pharynx is a continuation of the oral cavity and functions to pass the bolus toward the laryngeal pharynx below.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pharynx -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Four arteries from each external carotid artery supply the pharynx with oxygen-rich blood which is the ascending pharyngeal, tonsilar (a branch of the facial artery), maxillary and lingual arteries. Venous drainage is formed by the pharyngeal veins which drain the pharynx into the internal jugular vein. The lymphatic drainage of the pharynx is direct drainage, which means that lymph passes directly toward the deep cervical lymph nodes (DCLNs). The deep cervical lymph nodes are the groups of lymph nodes located along the course of the internal jaguar vein or indirect drainage toward the deep cervical lymph nodes via the retropharyngeal lymph nodes (located behind the pharynx) or paratracheal lymph nodes (alongside the course of the trachea). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Airway -- Structure and Function -- Upper airway
retrieved_docs.content: The upper airway extends from the nares and oral cavity to the level of the larynx and is lined predominantly by mucous membrane. The region's central conduit, the pharynx, occupies the space between the skull base and the esophagus and is divided into 3 continuous segments (see Image . Tonsils and Throat). The nasopharynx extends posteriorly from the nares and nasal cavity, with its superior limit at the cranial base and its inferior border defined by the palate, which separates it from the oropharynx. The oropharynx lies between the palate and the hyoid bone and is demarcated anteriorly from the oral cavity by the tonsillar arch. Inferior to this component, the hypopharynx provides continuity with both the esophagus and the larynx, beginning below the level of the hyoid bone.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lymph Nodes -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Level VII: Prevertebral Compartment Group, including Levels VIIa and VIIbLevel VIIa: Retropharyngeal Nodes
retrieved_docs.content: Anatomy These nodes are contained in the retropharyngeal space. They are divided into medial and lateral subgroups. The lateral groups are bounded superiorly by the superior edge of the C1 vertebral body, or the hard palate, and inferiorly by the superior edge of the body of the hyoid bone. Anteriorly, they are bounded by the posterior edge of the superior/middle pharyngeal constrictor muscles. They are bordered posteriorly by the longus capitis and longus colli muscles, laterally by the medial edge of the internal carotid artery, and medially by a virtual line parallel to the lateral edge of the longus capitis muscle. The medial groups are approximated at the midline and not well-defined.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Structure and Function -- Laryngeal Folds and Membranes
retrieved_docs.content: The aryepiglottic folds extend over the lateral aspects of the epiglottic, cuneiform, corniculate, and arytenoid cartilages, marking the entrance to the laryngeal lumen. The piriform sinuses lie just lateral to these folds, which form their medial borders and are sometimes called the "lateral food channels." The aryepiglottic folds, along with their associated cartilages, create a protective ring that helps prevent food from entering the laryngeal aditus. This ring varies in height, with a lower dorsal region known as the interarytenoid notch, which increases susceptibility to food or liquid incursions.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ear Eustachian Tube -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Both segments of the PM travel laterally and inferiorly to anchor to the rear and inferior medial surface of the angle of the mandible. The mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve innervates the PM. An additional function of these dual masticatory muscles is to enable peristaltic movement of the tube, in cooperative interaction with the LVPM and TVP, during the process of swallowing, mimicking the same active motions of the LP, even when there is no swallowing, allowing the tube to open. Their intervention changes the shape of the tube, which becomes more convex, allowing the muscles that act directly on the tube to provide a greater force. The PM is also useful, in particular, because it may act as a fulcrum so that the musculature of the tube acts as a more efficient vector.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Mask Airway -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Supraglottic devices, like the LMA, are designed to be inserted blindly through the mouth and into the hypopharynx to seal around the glottic opening allowing for ventilation. The mnemonic RODS can be used to predict difficulty in either placing an extraglottic device or in providing adequate gas exchange through one. RODS stands for Restriction , Obstruction/Obesity , Disrupted or Distorted anatomy , and Short thyromental distance .  Restriction refers to both increased airway resistance - extraglottic devices have lower leak pressures than endotracheal tubes - as well as restricted mouth opening insufficient to allow for the passage of the device. Patients with upper or lower airway obstruction due to a tumor or foreign body may make passage of or ventilation through the device difficult or impossible. Obese patients may have redundant tissue, making it more difficult to seat the device in place, and the increased ventilatory pressures required in obese patients may increase the likelihood of a leak. Distortion or disruption of the airway from the midline makes the device less likely to seat properly. Small mandibular spaces, assessed by shortened thyromental distance, are associated with the difficult use of extraglottic devices due to the position of the tongue.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Tonsils -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The lingual tonsil lies in the submucosa of the posterior 3rd of the tongue and consists of nodular lymphoid tissue forming the midline component of the Waldeyer ring at the oropharyngeal level. The pharyngeal tonsil is situated in the roof of the nasopharynx and consists of submucosal lymphoid tissue collections. The tubal tonsils, positioned laterally, surround the pharyngeal openings of the eustachian tubes and complete the ring.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Ascending Palatine Artery -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Blood Supply
retrieved_docs.content: The blood supply begins in the external carotid artery from which the facial artery branches. The facial artery then gives rise to the ascending palatine artery which then anastomoses with the tonsillar artery, ascending pharyngeal artery, and the descending palatine artery. The blood then continues to supply the soft palate; palatoglossus, palatopharyngeus, tensor veli palatini, musculus uvulae, and the levator veli palatine, as well as the eustachian tube, and palatine tonsils. [1] [2] The prestyloid compartment, also known as the parapharyngeal space, receives the majority of its blood supply from the ascending palatine artery. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2518
retrieved_docs.content: The brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway is identified as the direct_procedure_site_of a ventricular shunt to nasopharynx, indicating that this neural fluid conduit serves as the anatomical location where a shunt is placed to divert cerebrospinal fluid from the ventricles into the nasopharyngeal region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1263
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly linked to the larynx, indicating that examinations and assessments targeting these structures focus on the laryngeal anatomy. The relationship identified as “indirect_procedure_site_of” connects the larynx to the broader category “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system,” meaning that the larynx can serve as an indirect site for non‑operative therapeutic interventions within the respiratory system. Within this category, the relationship “has child” designates the specific procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway) as a subordinate or child procedure. Consequently, the placement of an oral/pharyngeal airway is classified under “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” and may involve the larynx indirectly. Recognizing these interconnected relationships aids clinicians in coordinating diagnostic evaluations of the larynx with therapeutic airway management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 624
retrieved_docs.content: The brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway serves as the direct procedural site for a ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx, indicating that this anatomical pathway is targeted when diverting cerebrospinal fluid toward the nasopharyngeal region. The clinical intervention described as insertion, replacement, or removal of an extracranial ventricular shunt is classified under the term تحويلة بطينية وعائية, linking the procedure to the broader category of ventricular‑atrial shunting techniques. This same intervention is closely related to, and may be synonymous with, the ventricular shunt to nasopharynx, suggesting that both phrases refer to similar surgical actions involving extracranial ventricular shunts. Together, these concepts connect the anatomical target (brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway), the specific shunt destination (nasopharynx), and the procedural classification (insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt) within the context of ventricular‑atrial shunt management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: The brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway serves as the direct procedural site for a ventricular shunt to nasopharynx. The shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is positioned within the broader category of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt and has a child procedure called التحويلة البطينية البريتونية (ventriculoperitoneal shunt). Additionally, the ventricular shunt to nasopharynx is related to, and possibly synonymous with, the general procedure of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt. These connections clarify the hierarchical classification and anatomical context of various extracranial ventricular shunt procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is categorized as a placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway (تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي). Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, and the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Injection of larynx includes, as a subordinate concept, the placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway, and the broader group of other non‑therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system also contains this airway‑placement procedure. Together, these connections highlight how airway management interventions such as intubation, mechanical ventilation, and oral‑pharyngeal airway placement are organized within the taxonomy of respiratory procedures, with the larynx functioning as a central anatomical focus for both diagnostic and non‑therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2333
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of the larynx includes the subsequent step of تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway), indicating that airway management is an integral component of the laryngeal injection procedure. This relationship underscores that when clinicians perform a laryngeal injection, they also consider establishing an oral‑pharyngeal airway to maintain airway patency and support effective ventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is recognized as a neurological disorder that involves the brain and nerves, and it is specifically identified within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway where excess cerebrospinal fluid accumulates. Clinically, hydrocephalus is associated with the development of dementia (خرف). In the management of hydrocephalus, shunting procedures are commonly employed; the ventricular‑peritoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) frequently co‑occurs with the condition and represents a specific type of shunt that diverts fluid from a cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site. This shunt is a child concept of the broader category “shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is nested under the procedural domain of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts. Another variant, the ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx, is performed directly on the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway and is considered closely related, and possibly synonymous, with the general insertion/replacement/removal procedure for extracranial ventricular shunts. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose hydrocephalus, anticipate its cognitive complications, and select appropriate shunting interventions for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly associated with the larynx, indicating that the larynx is the primary anatomical focus of these examinations. In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 54
retrieved_docs.content: “Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term)” and linked to the parent concept “Brain and Nerves.” Its primary anatomic site is the “Brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway,” which also serves as the procedural site for interventions such as “Ventricular shunt to nasopharynx.” Hydrocephalus commonly co‑occurs with a ventriculoperitoneal shunt (“التحويلة البطينية البريتونية”) and is clinically associated with general and senile dementia, underscoring its impact on cognitive function. Ventricular shunting procedures are hierarchically organized: “Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site” is a child of the specific shunt “التحويلة البطينية البريتونية” and of the broader category “insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt,” while “Ventricular shunt to nasopharynx” is related or synonymous with that broader category. Overall, the network provides a structured, multilingual representation of radiologic and neurosurgical procedures—ranging from basic X‑ray

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect site for various other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Within this broader category, the specific intervention described as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway) is classified as a child procedure. Recognizing that the larynx may be indirectly involved in such non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, including the insertion of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is important for clinicians when assessing airway management and potential procedural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2161
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the larynx and trachea are classified as a specific subset within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures of the respiratory tract and mediastinum, indicating that examinations targeting the laryngeal and tracheal structures belong to the larger group of diagnostic interventions performed on the respiratory system and mediastinal region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2313
retrieved_docs.content: The brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway serves as the direct procedural site for a ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx. The ventricular shunt to nasopharynx procedure is closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the insertion, replacement, or removal of an extracranial ventricular shunt, indicating that surgical management of this shunt involves placement, revision, or extraction within the cerebrospinal fluid pathway.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are categorized as a sub‑type of عمليات الأنف, indicating that they fall within the broader group of nose‑related surgical interventions. These diagnostic procedures are closely linked to منظار داخلي, an internal endoscopic technique that is commonly employed to visualize and assess the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and pharyngeal structures. Understanding that diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx belong to عمليات الأنف and utilize منظار داخلي helps clinicians select appropriate endoscopic tools for accurate evaluation and management of upper airway conditions.
